rag.description: The UndoNavigation plugin navigates the browser backward in session history. It supports configurable repeat counts and optional delays between steps, enabling controlled multi-step backward navigation in RPA workflows and automated test suites.

qa.question: What does the UndoNavigation plugin do?
qa.answer: It navigates the browser backward in session history, equivalent to clicking the browser's back button. The repeat count and inter-step delay are both configurable.

qa.question: What parameters does UndoNavigation support?
qa.answer: It supports Repeat (number of backward steps, default 1) and Delay (pause between steps in milliseconds or time format, default 0).

qa.question: How is UndoNavigation integrated into a Sequential Workflow?
qa.answer: It is used as an Action task node and maps to the W3C WebDriver POST /session/{session id}/back endpoint.

qa.question: What is the recommended approach for multi-step backward navigation?
qa.answer: Pass `{{$ --Repeat:N --Delay:Ms}}` as the argument to control the number of steps and the pause between them in a single action.

qa.question: What delay formats does the Delay parameter accept?
qa.answer: Delay accepts a millisecond integer such as 1000 or a time span string such as 00:00:01. Values exceeding the maximum integer millisecond range are reset to zero with a warning.

qa.question: What happens when UndoNavigation is called with no back history available?
qa.answer: The WebDriver back call completes without error but has no visible effect if no back history exists in the current session.

qa.question: What is the manifest version for UndoNavigation?
What is the purpose of UndoNavigation according to its description?
qa.answer: It automates backward browser navigation with configurable repeat counts and delays to support multi-step RPA workflows and browser state management in automated tests.

description: ### Purpose
The `UndoNavigation` plugin automates backward browser navigation, equivalent to clicking the browser's built-in back button.
It provides a structured way to traverse session history backward with configurable repeat counts and optional delays between steps.
The plugin supports both simple single-step navigation and multi-step sequences with per-iteration timing control.
### Key Features and Functionality
| Feature             | Description                                                                                                                  |
| Dynamic Repeats     | Configures how many times the browser navigates back in a single action invocation.                                          |
| Configurable Delays | Introduces a pause between each backward step, in milliseconds or time span format, to control navigation pace.              |
| Legacy Argument     | Accepts a plain integer directly as the repeat count for backwards-compatible usage.                                         |
| Delay Safeguard     | Logs a warning and resets the delay to zero when the configured value exceeds the maximum allowed integer millisecond range. |
### Usages in RPA
| Use Case             | Description                                                                                                           |
| Multi-Step Traversal | Navigates back through a sequence of previously visited pages in a single action without repeated manual back clicks. |
| Error Recovery       | Returns the browser to a known stable state before retrying a failed automation sequence.                             |
### Usages in Automation Testing
| Use Case                | Description                                                                                                        |
| Browser History Testing | Verifies that the browser correctly restores pages when navigating backward after a programmatic forward sequence. |
| Navigation Flow Testing | Simulates realistic back navigation patterns with controlled delays for performance test accuracy.                 |

edge_cases: Already at the beginning of back history — back call completes without error but has no visible effect
Browser session ends before navigation completes — WebDriver exception propagates to the caller
Page load triggered by the back step exceeds the implicit wait — downstream steps may interact with a stale or loading DOM
Single-page application updates URL state without a full reload — back navigation behaviour may differ from a traditional page
annotations.expected_result: The browser navigates backward by one step in session history.
annotations.notes: When no argument or parameters are supplied the plugin navigates back exactly once with no delay. This is equivalent to a single back button click.

description: ### Default single backward navigation
Navigates the browser backward by one step using default settings.
No repeat count or delay is configured, so the action executes once immediately.

edge_cases: Argument value is negative — treated as zero; no navigation steps are executed
Argument value is zero — no navigation steps are executed
Back history has fewer pages than the requested repeat count — remaining steps have no effect but complete without error
Page load between repeats takes longer than expected — the next step may act on a still-loading page
annotations.expected_result: The browser navigates backward three times in succession with no delay between steps.
annotations.notes: Passing a plain integer as the argument is the legacy shorthand for the Repeat count. It is equivalent to `{{$ --Repeat:3}}` but requires no parameter expression syntax.

description: ### Backward navigation using an integer argument
Navigates the browser backward three times by passing `3` as a plain integer argument.
The integer is interpreted directly as the repeat count without requiring parameter expression syntax.

edge_cases: Delay is zero but Repeat is greater than one — steps execute as fast as the browser allows with no pause
Delay value exceeds int.MaxValue milliseconds — a warning is logged and the delay resets to zero
Back history is exhausted before all repeats complete — remaining steps have no visible effect but do not throw
Network latency causes page loads to exceed the configured delay — the next back step may begin before the current page finishes loading
Time format string is malformed — ConvertToTimeSpan returns zero and the delay is effectively disabled
annotations.expected_result: The browser navigates backward three times, pausing 1000 milliseconds between each step.
annotations.notes: The `{{$ --Repeat:3 --Delay:1000}}` expression sets both the repeat count and the inter-step delay in milliseconds. The Delay parameter also accepts a time span string such as `00:00:01` for the same one-second pause.

description: ### Backward navigation with repeat count and delay
Navigates the browser backward three times, pausing 1000 milliseconds between each step.
The `{{$ --Repeat:3 --Delay:1000}}` argument expression sets both the repeat count and the inter-step delay in a single value.
Controls the pause between each backward navigation step.
Accepts a value in milliseconds or a time span string such as `00:00:01` for one second.
When the configured delay exceeds the maximum allowed integer millisecond value, a warning is logged and the delay resets to zero.
parameters.name: Delay

description: Sets how many times the browser navigates backward in one action invocation.
Useful when traversing multiple pages back is required without issuing separate back commands.
When no valid value is provided, the browser navigates backward exactly once.
parameters.name: Repeat

description: Provides the primary input for the backward navigation action.
Accepts either a plain integer as the repeat count or a parameter expression such as `{{$ --Repeat:3 --Delay:1000}}` for combined repeat and delay control.
When a plain integer is supplied and no `Repeat` parameter is present, the integer is used directly as the repeat count.
properties.name: Argument

summary: The `UndoNavigation` plugin navigates the browser backward in session history, equivalent to clicking the browser's built-in back button.
It supports configurable repeat counts and optional delays between steps, enabling controlled multi-step backward navigation in automation workflows.
Common uses include returning to a previous page in RPA flows and managing browser state during end-to-end testing.
